checks.a_analysis: The output content accurately reflects the source document. No fabricated paragraphs or sentences were identified. All headings, bullet points, and legal text match the source material. AI-generated elements like table captions and metadata titles are within acceptable changes.

The document contains minimal contact information (physical address for the Department of Justice). This address (Raleigh, North Carolina) is correctly transcribed. No phone numbers, URLs, or emails were present in the source or fabricated in the output.
All 5 pages and all primary sections (APPEARANCES, ISSUE, STATUTES AT ISSUE, WITNESSES, EXHIBITS, FINDINGS OF FACT, CONCLUSIONS OF LAW, DECISION, NOTICE) are present in the output JSON.

The source document uses a layout table for the case caption on Page 1. This is represented in the JSON as a single-row table with two cells, which captures the petitioner/respondent information and the 'FINAL DECISION' label correctly.

checks.a_analysis: The output JSON represents the content of the five-page document without any observed duplication of paragraphs, headings, or list items. Each section of the legal decision appears exactly once, following the logical flow of the original document.

The heading hierarchy is consistent. Main sections (APPEARANCES, ISSUE, STATUTES AT ISSUE, WITNESSES, EXHIBITS, FINDINGS OF FACT, CONCLUSIONS OF LAW, DECISION, NOTICE) are correctly tagged as H2. Sub-sections within EXHIBITS are correctly tagged as H3. While no H1 is present in the document body, the metadata contains the document title, and the use of H2 for top-level sections is a standard normalization practice for document fragments or specific page content.

The document contains one complex structural element: the case caption box on page 1. The pipeline has correctly converted this into a table structure with two cells representing the left (parties) and right (decision type) sides of the box. The headers and cell contents are appropriately handled for accessibility.

The reading order in the JSON follows the source document exactly. It begins with the court/county header, proceeds through the case caption, the introductory 'THIS MATTER' paragraph, and then follows the headings and numbered lists sequentially through the five pages to the final signature block.

The numbered lists for 'FINDINGS OF FACT' and 'CONCLUSIONS OF LAW' are correctly converted into ordered list structures. All items (1-15 for Findings and 1-5 for Conclusions) are preserved and appropriately nested within their respective list elements.

No links are injected or missing. The source document contains text references to statutes and case law, but no clickable hyperlinks. The JSON appropriately treats these as standard text within paragraphs and list items.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML document faithfully reproduces the visual organization of the source PDF. The multi-column header on page 1 (State/County/Office/Case No) is logically linearized. The case caption box is rendered as a clean table, maintaining the visual relationship between the parties and the 'FINAL DECISION' label. The progression of sections (APPEARANCES, ISSUE, STATUTES, WITNESSES, etc.) follows the source exactly. The use of a single-column layout with a navigation sidebar is an expected responsive HTML adaptation and does not compromise the visual integrity of the document's flow.

The primary layout 'table' (the case caption box on page 1) is rendered correctly in the HTML under the 'Case Caption and Decision Type' section. It uses a structured table layout that keeps the Petitioner/Respondent info separate from the 'FINAL DECISION' label, just as in the PDF. No other tables are present in the source document.
The source PDF is a text-based legal document and does not contain any meaningful images, logos, seals, or diagrams. No visual assets are missing in the HTML output.

There is no visual duplication of body content. While section headings appear in both the 'Contents' sidebar and the main body, this is an intentional accessible design feature for navigation and is not considered a defect. The actual text paragraphs, findings of fact, and conclusions of law appear exactly once in the rendered output.
